title: Programming nanomechanical computation with light
authors: Xiaofei Guo, Jonne Drost, Fons van der Laan, Jesse J. Slim, Marc Serra-Garcia, Ewold Verhagen
abstract: Looking at physical systems as computers allows us to regard physical properties, such as thermal noise, symmetry or topology, as unconventional resources for computation. However, harnessing these resources requires programming computational functionality through strong, controllable nonlinearities in the system. Here, we show that cavity optomechanical interactions allow laser-controlled computation with nanomechanical degrees of freedom. We demonstrate a set of basic digital logic gates with level restoration and controlled mechanical couplings as essential ingredients for arbitrary computing networks. Owing to the strong optomechanical nonlinearity and precise readout, the system operates close to thermal amplitudes, in the regime where thermodynamic stochasticity governs its behavior. This opens a new path for the realization of physical computing with controlled nonlinear resonators.
summary: The researchers have created a programmable computing platform using nanomechanical resonators where the logic functions and connections are controlled by light. By tuning lasers, they can switch between different Boolean gates and even connect different mechanical modes. This work shows that optomechanical nonlinearities can be harnessed to create reconfigurable, hardware-agnostic physical computers.
main_problem: Developing a programmable physical computing platform where nonlinearity and connectivity can be reconfigured in situ, rather than being fixed by material properties.
main_result: Demonstration of all six fundamental Boolean logic gates (AND, NAND, OR, NOR, XOR, XNOR) and programmable connectivity between mechanical modes using laser-controlled optomechanical interactions.
method: Using cavity optomechanical backaction to induce Duffing nonlinearity and an optical spring effect, controlled via laser wavelength, power, and phase, with readout via intensity modulation detection.
model_or_system: A pair of silicon nanobeams acting as mechanical oscillators coupled to a photonic-crystal nanocavity, modeled as a Duffing oscillator with radiation-pressure-induced nonlinearity.
key_observables: Mechanical oscillation amplitude, frequency response (Lorentzian vs. shark-fin), power spectral density (PSD), and gate fidelity/error rates.
important_parameters: Mechanical frequency (5-18 MHz), optical detuning, Duffing nonlinearity coefficient (beta), damping rate (Gamma), and thermal amplitude (x_th).
main_assumptions: Use of the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) for single-mode treatment and neglecting higher-order harmonics and intermodulation products through optimized DC bias.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup and the transition from linear to nonlinear frequency response. Figure 2 demonstrates the realization of all six logic gates. Figure 3 shows the implementation of cascadable gates using multiple eigenmodes. Figure 4 shows the time trace and noise analysis of an OR gate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of programmable physical computing, describes the optomechanical platform and the Duffing model, demonstrates the implementation of various logic gates, shows the extension to multi-mode cascading, and concludes with a detailed noise and error rate analysis.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a way to use classical nonlinear dynamics and optomechanical backaction to implement programmable logic, which provides a bridge to understanding how controlled nonlinearities can be used for more complex tasks like reservoir computing or probabilistic computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Computation and programmable nonlinearity enabled by optomechanical interactions. a. Schematic of computation using an oscillator network. A programmable response of individual oscillators and their interactions enables the network output (oscillator motion) to be tuned to a desired target under a prescribed external-force input. b. Optomechanical system used to realize the oscillator network. A nanobeam (gray) serves as the mechanical oscillators, the second eigenmode (green) is read out as the output. The inputs are provided by signal lasers with intensity modulation at Ωd = Ωm + δΩ+ δΩd, and a function laser programs the properties of individual oscillators. Here δΩdenotes the...

FIG. 2. Reconfigurable implementation of six standard Boolean operations in a single device. a. The device function is reconfigured by three control parameters: the laser wavelength λ, the modulator phase, and the modulation am- plitude bias. The modulation bias is applied with 180◦phase. b. Without redefining the input and output convention, we experimentally realize six standard Boolean operations by appropriately tuning these three parameters.

FIG. 3. Cascadable logic gates using multiple mechanical eigenmodes. a. Schematic illustration of cascadable logic gates. Three mechanical eigenmodes of the nanobeam are employed, with two modes serving as input modes A and B and the third mode as the output mode. In addition to the signal lasers and the function laser used in the single-mode computation, an extra coupling laser is applied to establish connections between the input and output modes. The coupling laser is intensity- modulated near both Ωout −ΩA and Ωout −ΩB. b. The mechanical oscillation amplitudes of the three eigenmodes when driving input mode A only, input mode B only, and both input modes A and B using an intensity...

FIG. 4. Computation fidelity near the thermal limit. a. Time trace of an OR gate output under a continuous input sequence. Since inputs 01 (10) and 11 both produce output bit 1, the output exhibits two mean levels. Data are acquired with the lock-in amplifier filter bandwidth set to 10 kHz. The mechanical oscillation amplitude is presented both in absolute units and normalized by the thermal displacement xth, providing a dimensionless measure relative to thermal motion. b. Power spectral density of the output signal in the undriven state (bit 0) and the driven state (bit 1), where the driven case corresponds to input 11.
